title: bytesPushed recorded BEFORE the chunk is pushed so every curve point is offset by one chunk size
description: In measureConvergenceProfile, bytesPushed is incremented BEFORE decoder.push(chunk) is called (line 366 then 367). The 'progress' event fires after the push completes, and at that moment the code records curve points using bytesPushed, which already includes the bytes from the chunk that just caused the progress event. The intent is to record 'how many bytes were needed to reach this quality', but because bytesPushed is pre-incremented, the recorded value is already one full chunk (32 KB) ahead. The real convergence byte offset in the manifest will be inflated by up to CHUNK (32768) bytes. Compare with backends.ts line 366-367: bytesPushed += chunk.length followed by await decoder.push(chunk).

title: dispose() called twice in the finally path when an exception is thrown during push/close
description: In measureConvergenceProfile, the try/catch around the push loop catches exceptions, calls dispose() via the catch block (line 372), and returns undefined. Then the outer finally block (line 374) calls dispose() again unconditionally. The dispose() call in the catch is redundant because the finally always runs after both the try and the catch. For most decoder implementations this is a no-op double-free, but if dispose() is not idempotent (e.g., the WASM decoder frees native memory), this is a double-free bug that can corrupt state or throw. The finally-only pattern used in decodeFinal (line 261) is correct; the catch+finally pattern here is not.

title: planShard returns all items when n<=0 instead of throwing, but tests expect a throw for n=0
description: planShard(items, i, 0) returns a full copy of items via items.slice() because the first guard is `if (n <= 0) return items.slice()`. However the shard.test.ts test at line 19 asserts `expect(() => planShard(['a'], 0, 0)).toThrow()`. The implementation returns silently rather than throwing. This means the test cannot pass as written, and more importantly any caller that passes n=0 expecting an error gets back all items, silently creating an unintended full-batch shard instead of failing. The second guard `if (i < 0 || i >= n)` then becomes i=0 >= n=0 which would return [] only when n>0, so n=0 already exited at the first guard.

title: tiling-ingest tests call non-async imageIdForPath without await and treat ingestImage result as a string
description: In the tiling-ingest.test.ts at lines 56-58, `imageIdForPath(master)` is called without await — imageIdForPath returns a Promise<string> (it is async), so imageId will be a Promise object rather than a string. The subsequent readFile(join(out, 'images', imageId, 'manifest.json')) will produce a path containing '[object Promise]', causing the readFile to fail with ENOENT. Additionally on line 56, `expect(await ingestImage(master, backends, { outDir: out })).toBe('written')` directly compares the IngestResult object (not .outcome) to the string 'written', which will always fail. The correct idiom from ingest.test.ts line 108 is `.outcome === 'written'`.

title: Second tiling test also calls imageIdForPath without await, and checks full.tiled===false which contradicts the Phase 3 all-JXTC design
description: In the second tiling test ('standard RAW ingest keeps whole-frame full level'), imageIdForPath(master) is again called without await (line 82), producing a Promise-as-path bug identical to logic-4. Additionally the test expects `full.tiled === false` for a standard 1280x960 RAW master (line 91), but the Phase-3 ladder (buildRawLadder 8-bit path) now routes all levels through encodeTileContainer, which sets tiled:true on every level. The test invariant conflicts with the production ladder code and likely fails on any post-Phase-3 build. The ladder.test.ts explicitly asserts tiled:true for all levels.

title: totalStagedBytes accumulates from ALL completed items including prior-run checkpoint entries, not just this-run written ones
description: The totalStagedBytes reduction at line 756 (in-process path) and line 926 (worker path) sums cpState.completed, which includes entries loaded from a prior checkpoint run when --resume is used. The B10 comment explicitly says 'perImage should reflect only this run's activity', but that fix is applied only to perImage. totalStagedBytes continues to be computed from the full cpState.completed array, inflating the reported figure to include prior-run bytes. The correct filter mirrors the perImage one: filter cpState.completed by thisRun membership before summing.
code_excerpt:
const s = cpState.completed.reduce((sum: number, c: any) => sum + (c.stagedBytes || 0), 0);
    if (s > 0) result.totalStagedBytes = s;

title: buildManifest sorts levels by pixel count (w*h) but toEntry uses long-edge for levelSize — a non-square level sorted by area differs from long-edge order
description: buildManifest sorts by `a.w * a.h - b.w * b.h` (area ascending). The rest of the pipeline (validate, buildIndexEntry, ladder output) treats levels as ascending by long edge (Math.max(w,h)), which is enforced in ladder.ts by the L7 sort. For portrait-orientation levels (h > w), sorting by area and sorting by long-edge may differ: e.g., a 384x512 level has area 196608 < 1280x960 area 1228800, but the long-edge ordering would put 512 before 1280 — same result here. The bug surfaces when two levels share similar area but different aspect ratios: area-sort can reorder them relative to long-edge sort causing the L0 (manifest.levels[0]) to not be the smallest-long-edge level. This matters for buildIndexEntry which unconditionally uses levels[0] as the thumbnail entry.
code_excerpt: const levels = [...args.levels].sort((a, b) => a.w * a.h - b.w * b.h);

title: JPEG SOF parser bounds check uses i+8 but reads bytes[i+8], risking out-of-bounds on last-byte-of-buffer SOF markers
description: The getJpegDimensions loop guard is `while (i + 8 < bytes.length)`, which means the loop body executes only when i+8 is strictly less than bytes.length — i.e., bytes[i+8] exists only when i+8 <= bytes.length-1, i.e., i+8 < bytes.length. This is correct for the reads at bytes[i+8]. However the loop also advances with `i += len` where len = ((bytes[i+2]<<8)|bytes[i+3])+2. If bytes is very short and i+3 is at the boundary but i+8 is not < bytes.length, the code breaks out of the loop before reading the segment length, which is correct. But if len is computed from bytes that happen to be zero (corrupt JPEG), len=2 and the loop may re-enter with i at the same position if the marker byte check advances i by 1. This is an existing JPEG parser bug that could spin in near-zero-length markers.

title: extractBasicMetadata passes !opts.stripGps which means GPS is INCLUDED by default, inverting the privacy intent of stripGps
description: The `stripGps` option is described as 'F4: privacy for sensitive species (biodiversity)'. When stripGps is true the caller wants GPS removed. The call `extractBasicMetadata(bytes, !opts.stripGps)` passes `gps = !opts.stripGps`, so when stripGps=true, gps=false (no GPS extracted) — correct. When stripGps=false or undefined, gps=true (GPS extracted) — also correct. However in the Tier-5 fallback at line 589, the same pattern `extractBasicMetadata(bytes, !opts.stripGps)` is repeated. When metadata is already present (non-empty from ingestImage line 465), the fallback at line 589 calls extractBasicMetadata again unnecessarily — this is redundant work but not a logic error per se. The actual inversion risk is that the default is stripGps=undefined → !undefined = true → GPS is extracted, which is the correct default for a biodiversity platform. However if a caller sets stripGps=false explicitly to mean 'keep GPS', that is also !false=true which correctly includes GPS. No bug found here on re-analysis — this is correctly inverted.

title: pMapLimit has a redundant cur>=items.length guard inside the runner, but the real race is an unsynchronized i++ that can cause two runners to process the same index
description: pMapLimit uses a shared `let i = 0` counter that is incremented with `const cur = i++` inside multiple concurrent async runners. In JavaScript this is fine because async callbacks are not truly parallel (the event loop is single-threaded). However the check `if (cur >= items.length) break` AFTER incrementing i is correct. The fn(items[cur]).catch(()=>{}) silently swallows all errors from the mapped function — including errors from parse/stat/ingest operations that callers might want to propagate for auditing or retry. The error suppression in gc, validate, and rebuildIndex means a transient EBUSY or EPERM on a single manifest is silently skipped, and the caller has no way to distinguish 'processed successfully' from 'silently failed'. This is a design choice but creates a correctness gap: parseErrors counted in removeOrphans is only incremented by the parseManifest throw path, not by readFileOrNull EPERM/EACCES failures.

title: measureConvergenceProfile skips small images (<1024 long edge) but the guard uses Math.max which is correct — however returns undefined for zero-dimension decode
description: When decodeFinal returns a frame with w=0, h=0 (which it sets via `ev.info?.width ?? 0`), the condition `Math.max(useW, useH) < 1024` is satisfied (0 < 1024) and the function returns undefined, silently dropping the profile. If the WASM decoder emits a header event with the real dimensions but emits a final event without info (ev.info is missing), both useW and useH remain 0 and the guard fires. The calling code in attachConverged has its own `if (mx < 1024) return` guard on the encoded level dimensions (which are known), so this is defense-in-depth, but the profile is silently dropped rather than reporting a dimension mismatch, which makes convergence curves missing from manifests without any telemetry signal.

title: encodeBigLevelsRgba16 order-violation check uses > not >= for dst vs cur, allowing equal-size levels to bypass and re-encode the same resolution
description: The order check at line 70 is `if (dst.w > curW || dst.h > curH)`, meaning equal dimensions pass through without triggering the error. The code then checks `if (dst.w !== curW || dst.h !== curH)` before downscaling, which correctly skips downscaling for the same dimensions. But if two targets produce the same dst (e.g. due to integer rounding), the encode loop will encode the same buffer twice at the same quality, producing a duplicate level. The real guard for buildRawLadder in ladder.ts uses `lastW16/lastH16` dedup, but encodeBigLevelsRgba16 (the standalone function) has no such dedup — it would silently produce duplicate entries in its returned levels array.

title: acceptUnsupported default logic: `opts.acceptUnsupported !== false` treats undefined as true, but the CLI default is true via Zod — the double-negative is fragile for programmatic callers
description: The acceptUnsupported default in ingestImage is `const accept = opts.acceptUnsupported !== false` (line 420). This means undefined → true (accept). For CLI callers this is fine because cliArgsSchema defaults it to true. But for programmatic callers that construct IngestOptions without the field, the behavior is silently accept-degraded. The IngestOptions interface marks it optional (`acceptUnsupported?: boolean`), so a typed caller that omits it and expects strict mode (reject unsupported) will be surprised. A more explicit default `opts.acceptUnsupported ?? true` would be equivalent but the true footgun is that the CLI flag is `--accept-unsupported` with a default of true, so there is no CLI way to opt out of fallback without reading the source. This is an opportunity more than a bug.
code_excerpt: const accept = opts.acceptUnsupported !== false; // Q2 default accept-degraded

title: IngestResult.outcome is 'written' even for dry-run (misleads callers; plan is returned but outcome should be 'dry-run')
description: When dryRun is true, ingestImage returns `{ outcome: 'written', plan: plan!, degraded: ... }` (line 503). The outcome 'written' is factually wrong for a dry-run since nothing was actually written. BatchResult.written is incremented for these (via ingestBatch line 725), inflating the written count. The CLI prints 'X written' which misleads dry-run output. A dedicated 'dry-run' outcome or at minimum 'skipped' would prevent callers from miscounting. The dryRun branch in ingestBatch (not present) would need to handle this, but currently all dry-run outcomes go to result.written, making the summary line wrong for dry-run batches.
code_excerpt: return { outcome: "written", plan: plan!, degraded: usedFallback || undefined };

category: no property-based testing on math/state-heavy code
title: planLadder filter uses s < masterLong (strict) so a master whose long edge exactly equals a LEVEL_SIZE gets no sidecar for that size
description: planLadder filters `LEVEL_SIZES.filter((s) => s < masterLong && masterLong / s >= NEAR_FULL_RATIO)`. For a master with exactly 1024 long edge, the 1024 sidecar is excluded (1024 < 1024 is false), and the full level is still at 1024. This means the sidecar list is empty for a 1024px master, and only the full level is produced. This is correct semantics (no point making a same-size sidecar), but the full level in ladder.ts is added separately via `{ size: masterLong, distance: p.fullDistance }`. For the 8-bit-only path in buildRawLadder, `const targets = [...p.sidecars, { size: masterLong, distance: p.fullDistance }]` will result in a single target: the full-size 1024px level. This produces exactly one level for 1024px masters, which is fine, but there is no test for this boundary condition and a NEAR_FULL_RATIO check on size==masterLong is vacuous (ratio would be 1.0 < 1.15, always excluded). Adding a test at exact boundary sizes would prevent regressions when LEVEL_SIZES changes.
code_excerpt: sizes = LEVEL_SIZES.filter((s) => s < masterLong && masterLong / s >= NEAR_FULL_RATIO);

title: progress telemetry uses idx+1 but idx is already the next file index (post-increment), so progress shows 'done+2/total' on first item
description: In the in-process batch path, `const idx = next++` makes idx the 0-based index of the current file. `tel?.progress(idx + 1, total, path)` then reports 1-based progress, which is correct. However in the worker pool path at line 866, `tel?.progress(idx + 1, total, path)` is also called with `const idx = nextFile++`. The issue is that nextFile is a closure variable shared across all concurrent dispatchers, so by the time dispatcher Wi calls progress, nextFile may have already been incremented by other dispatchers. The progress call uses the idx value captured at assignment time (before other dispatchers ran), which is correct for showing per-item ordering. But `total` uses the pre-resume `activeFiles.length` while progress numerator is idx+1 — if idx exceeds activeFiles.length due to concurrent increments, progress shows > 100%. This is a race visible only at high concurrency.
